title: Filling scratches and dents with wax sticks
subtitle: Match the colour, press fill in, pare flush
excerpt: How to fill scratches, gouges, and small dents in painted or polished furniture using furniture wax fill sticks: selecting the matching colour, pressing the wax into the damage, paring flush, and buffing to blend with the surrounding surface.
sourceNotes: Furniture repair practice. Guild of Master Craftsmen guide to furniture finishing (2001). English Heritage guidance on surface repair of finished furniture.

content.text: Furniture wax fill sticks are the fastest repair for scratches, small gouges, and dents in finished timber. The wax is softer than the surrounding finish, so it can be pressed in and pared flush without additional tools. The repair is not structural but it is invisible when the colour is matched correctly.
Selecting the colour
Step 1, match the wax colour to the finish.

Hold each stick from the set against the undamaged surface in daylight, not artificial light. The wax should match the mid-tone of the surrounding timber, not the lightest or darkest part of the grain. On timber with strong grain variation, blending two sticks together by rubbing them at the tip produces intermediate colours. Test the blend on a piece of card before applying to the furniture.
Filling the damage
Step 2, clean the scratch or dent.

Wipe the area with a dry cloth to remove dust and loose finish. Any wax conditioner or furniture polish already in the scratch will prevent the fill wax from bonding; clean it out with a dry cotton bud before filling.
Step 3, press the wax into the damage.

Rub the wax stick tip directly over the scratch with moderate pressure. The friction softens the wax slightly so it flows into the damage. Overfill the repair slightly; the wax should stand slightly proud of the surface so there is material to pare back. Do not scrub back and forth; work across the damage in short passes.
Step 4, pare flush.

Hold the scraper at a very low angle (around 10 degrees to the surface) and draw it across the filled area with one clean stroke. The goal is to take off only the proud wax without scratching the finish around it. A sharp plastic credit card or a piece of hard card can substitute for the scraper on delicate or thin finishes. Wipe the levelled area with a clean cloth.
Finishing
Step 5, buff to blend.

Rub the repair with a clean soft cloth using small circular movements, then finishing along the grain. This polishes the wax surface to a sheen that matches the surrounding finish. On a matt surface, avoid buffing too vigorously; the wax will burnish brighter than the surrounding timber and become visible.
Step 6, check in raking light.

Hold a torch or desk lamp at a very low angle across the repair. Raking light shows any remaining proud wax, missed areas, or colour mismatch that direct light conceals. Pare and re-buff any high spots before declaring the repair complete.
Common mistakes
Wax repair visible as a shiny patch on a matt surface.
The repair was buffed too hard. Wax takes a high shine with friction; on a satin or matt finish, dab the repair gently with a dry cloth rather than rubbing, to avoid burnishing.
Wax sinks below the surface after a day.

The damage was deeper than it appeared; the wax compressed under the scraper and then relaxed slightly overnight. Refill with a second application, pressing more firmly and overfilling to a slightly greater height before paring.
